ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for smoked beef in coconut milk gravy (Malay regional preparation). Mapped to closest parent 'beef dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for smoked beef in spiced coconut milk. A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest parent FoodEx2 anchor. Dish-level specificity is not available in the FoodEx2 catalogue for this traditional Malay preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific traditional Malay smoked beef dish.

preparation_method: slow-smoked over wood fire or charcoal

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Daging Salai Masak Lemak Cili Api is approximately 250 g, comprising smoked beef pieces in a generous portion of spicy yellow coconut milk gravy, typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) — Beef and Coconut Milk Dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Daging Salai Masak Lemak Cili Api exists in MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for cooked beef dishes with coconut milk (masak lemak style), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for smoked beef and coconut milk components. The input enriched_json values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution using USDA FoodData Central coconut milk data (SR Legacy #12117). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available in the adapted source combination and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; analogous estimate based on high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate beef and coconut milk dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Daging Salai Masak Lemak Cili Api. GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate beef and coconut milk dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. The high protein (14.2 g/100 g) and fat (14.5 g/100 g) content from beef and coconut milk substantially slow gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Carbohydrate content is only 2.8 g/100 g with no added sugars. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 7.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.5 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (310 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from thick coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g per meal, request reduced salt and diluted coconut milk during preparation, and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake, as a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 775 mg sodium — over one-third of the recommended daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~35) due to high protein and fat from beef and coconut milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Daging Salai Masak Lemak Cili Api is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its very low carbohydrate load (glycemic load ~2.5 per 250 g serving) produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion; however, total caloric density and saturated fat intake should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~9.8 g/100 g) from thick coconut milk and smoked beef, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions (≤100 g), as the high saturated fat from coconut milk raises LDL cholesterol risk and the elevated sodium contributes to blood pressure burden; substituting light coconut milk and reducing salt can partially mitigate these risks.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric (curcumin) and chili (capsaicin) are beneficial, but high saturated fat and smoked compounds may be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh turmeric and bird's eye chili in this dish provide curcumin and capsaicin with documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, the high saturated fat content and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from the smoking process may counteract these benefits in individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Daging Salai Masak Lemak Cili Api is a celebrated traditional main dish from Negeri Sembilan, Malaysia, and a hallmark of the Minangkabau-influenced Malay culinary tradition of that state. The dish features beef that has been slow-smoked ('salai') over a wood fire or charcoal until deeply aromatic and tender, then sliced and simmered in a thin but intensely flavored yellow gravy made from thick coconut milk (santan pekat), freshly pounded turmeric (kunyit hidup), and a generous quantity of bird's eye chilies (cili api). Bruised lemongrass (serai) and dried tamarind slices (asam keping) are added to the gravy for fragrance and a characteristic gentle sourness. A defining feature of this regional style is that no oil is used to sauté the spice paste — the coconut milk itself provides the cooking medium, resulting in a lighter yet richly flavored sauce. The dish delivers a complex sensory profile: intense smokiness from the beef, fiery heat from the chilies, earthy warmth from the turmeric, and a creamy richness from the coconut milk balanced by tamarind acidity. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 195 kcal, 14.2 g protein, 14.5 g fat (predominantly saturated from coconut milk, estimated ~9.8 g), and only 2.8 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, high-fat, very low-carbohydrate dish. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), with a glycemic load of only ~2.5 per standard 250 g serving. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its minimal carbohydrate impact, but should be consumed cautiously by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium (~310 mg/100 g) and saturated fat content. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is traditionally served with steamed white rice at lunch or dinner and is a source of iron (1.8 mg/100 g), zinc (2.2 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg/100 g), and niacin (4.5 mg/100 g) from the beef component.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Daging Salai Masak Lemak Cili Api exists in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD cooked beef and coconut milk dish entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. The input enriched_json values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) are consistent with this adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Saturated fat is estimated from coconut milk contribution. Several micronutrient fields (-1) reflect genuine data gaps in the adapted source combination.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for this traditional smoked beef and coconut milk preparation are not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific dish combination as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (9.8 g/100 g) is estimated from the coconut milk contribution using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #12117 (Coconut milk, raw) proportioned to the dish composition. 3. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The input source citation ('Nutrient Analysis of Negeri Sembilan Traditional Smoked Dishes', Health Promotion Board Singapore) could not be independently verified as a published HPB document; values have been cross-validated against IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy and found consistent. 5. This dish is a Malaysian regional specialty; multilingual coverage is provided in Malay only, as Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable for this non-Singaporean dish. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied for Malaysian Malay cuisine (Malay name required and present). 6. Ontology match types corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
